rulings.Case Title: JOHN WILKINS VS. MARY BAYTOP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause Why Order Of Sale Of Dwelling Should Not Be Made
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, March 28, 2014, line 2. Order To Show Cause Why Order Of Sale Of Dwelling Should Not Be Made. Denied, without prejudice. The motion is procedurally deficient. First, Mr. Wilkins did not timely file proof of service demonstrating "[personal service] of each document listed in [CCP § 704.770(b)(1)] on an occupant of the dwelling or, if there is no occupant present at the time service is attempted, post[ing] a copy of each document in a conspicuous place at the dwelling" pursuant to CCP § 704.770(b)(2). (Cal. Rules of Court, rule 3.1300(c).) Mr. Wilkins has demonstrated compliance with CCP § 704.770(b)(1) only, and not CCP § 704.770(b)(2). (See Proof of Service, filed Jan. 9, 2014.) Second, in his application, Mr. Wilkins has not averred under oath the amount of any liens or encumbrances on the dwelling and the name and address of each person have a lien or encumbrance. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: PATRICIA J WEISS PHD VS. BOARD OF PSYCHOLOGY, STATE OF CALIFORNIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Adminstrative Writ
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, March 28, 2014, line 17. PETITIONER PATRICIA WEISS' Petition For Adminstrative Writ. Granted. Although Dr. Weiss has not established that the administrative finding that she violated Bus. & Prof. Code secs. 2960(n) and (p) is contrary to the weight of the evidence, Dr. Weiss has established that the evidence does not support the other administrative findings. As to Bus. & Prof. Code sec. 2960(k), the evidence does not support the finding that Dr. Weiss violated Ethical Standards 3.10, 3.07, and 9.01. Dr. Weiss had a good faith belief that the study was Court approved based on the email from attorney Kristina Kliszewski stating that the Court approved funding. The evidence is ambiguous as to whether Dr. Weiss attempted to contact the social worker. Additionally, there is diametrically different expert testimony as to whether Dr. Weiss' observations were sufficient to substantiate her findings. Further, Dr. Weiss has established that she may not be subject to discipline for violating Ethical Principle C, as the American Psychological Association Guidelines explicitly state that "General Principles, in contrast to Ethical Standards, do not represent obligations and should not form the basis for imposing sanctions." As to Bus. & Prof. Code sec. 2960(j), the evidence does not support a finding that Dr. Weiss' conduct was "an extreme departure from the ordinary standard of conduct or the lack of even scant care." (Kearl v. Board of Medical Quality Assurance (1986) 189 Cal.App.3d 1040, 1052.) Dr. Weiss has established that the imposition of the maximum penalty, revocation of her license, was a manifest abuse of discretion. (Landau v. Medical Board (1988) 81 Cal.App.4th 191, 218.) The evidence establishes that Dr. Weiss has never been the subject of other disciplinary proceedings, Dr. Weiss will not perform another bonding study in the future, and Dr. Weiss acted on a good faith belief that there was a court order to perform the bonding study. Further, no evidence was presented that even if the bonding study would have been admitted into evidence by the court, harm would have come to the children. (See generally Magit v. Board of Medical Examiners (1961) 57 Cal.2d 74.) The matter is remanded to the Board of Psychology to set aside its order of revocation and impose appropriate discipline consistent with this order. The discipline imposed shall not exceed probation. rulings.Case Title: MARGARITA HERRERA VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production, Emplouyment Form Interrogatories, Requests For Admission And For An Award Of A Monetary Sanctions Against Deft And Its Counsel In The Amount Of $2040.00
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, March 28, 2014, Line 3, PLAINTIFF MARGARITA HERRERA's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Production, Employment Form Interrogatories, Requests For Admission And For An Award Of A Monetary Sanctions Against Deft And Its Counsel In The Amount Of $2040.00 Pro Tem Judge Bruce Highman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Motion re general objections denied. 201.1-Defendant shall identify the pages by Bates #. 215.1, 215.2-Hearing required. If Plaintiff agrees not to ask or refer at trial re any investigation other than the union grievance investigation or investigations which Defendant declines to agree not to introduce or refer to, Defendant will answer the interrogatories except re interviews or statements that were part of investigations which defendant agrees it will not introduce or refer to. Request 1-Objections overruled. Defendant will amend response to: "The City is producing plaintiff's entire personnel file." Files on Plaintiff maintained onsite or by her supervisor will be produced if not already produced. 6, 13, 14, 15, 16-A privilege log is due by 4/14. If the log doesn't satisfy P, the parties will meet and confer. If the matter isn't resolved, then by 5/8, Plaintiff will file supplemental papers. Defendant will file a response by 5/22. Copies will be served on the pro tem. He will hold a phone hearing. The objection as to "refer" or "pertain" is sustained. The words "Subject to . . . Objections" will be deleted. The sentences saying what is being produced will be amended to: "The City is producing all nonprivileged documents that relate to: [6] all complaints that plaintiff has ever lodged concerning her treatment by defendant or any of its employees; [13] plaintiff's dismissal in June 2012 from the nursing position at San Francisco General Hospital; [14] defendant's efforts to determine if plaintiff was a qualified person with a disability at any time during her employment with defendant; [15] defendant's efforts to engage in the interactive process with plaintiff at any time during her employment with defendant; [16] defendant's efforts to offer and/or provide plaintiff any form of reasonable accommodation at any time during her employment with defendant." Motion re admissions denied. Further responses/production due by 4/14. Sanctions denied. =302/jpt

rulings.Case Title: NICK C. GRAY et al VS. MARGARET L. NARRON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Herman Frank'S Motion To Strike 1st Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, March 28, 2014, line 16. Defendant Herman Frank's Motion To Strike 1st Amended Complaint. Granted as to the second cause of action for negligence, and denied as to the first cause of action for malicious prosecution. It is undisputed that this action derives from prior litigation, and thus Defendant Franck has met his initial burden of proof. "Litigation is activity protected by the speech and petition clauses." (Drummond v. Desmarais (2009) 176 Cal.App.4th 439, 449.) For the first cause of action, Plaintiffs have met their shifting burden showing a likelihood of prevailing on the merits for malicious prosecution. (See Contemporary Services Corp. v. Staff Pro Inc. (2007) 152 Cal.App.4th 1043, 1056.) Plaintiffs obtained a favorable determination of the merits in Narron v. Gray (case no. CUD-12-641735). The entry of judgment was based on the substantive merits; Defendant Narron was not a proper plaintiff to seek the eviction of Plaintiffs. Defendants lacked probable cause in filing the underlying action. Plaintiffs have demonstrated malice by Defendants in their continued prosecution of the underlying action despite knowing that Defendant Narron was not a proper plaintiff. For the second cause of action, the negligence claim is barred by the litigation privilege. (Civ. Code § 47(b).) "The only exception to application of section 47[(b)] to tort suits has been for malicious prosecution actions." (Silberg v. Anderson (1990) 50 Cal.3d 205, 215.)
